html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子不语 - Title meaning 'Confucius does not speak about' or 'Confucius does not discuss.'
  • 怪、力、乱、神 - Refers to strange, violent, disorderly, and superstitious matters, which Confucius is said to avoid discussing.
  • 龙血 - Literally 'dragon blood,' likely a reference to a mythical or legendary substance.
  • 鬼车 - Refers to a mythical creature, often translated as 'ghost carriage' or 'ghost chariot.'
  • 牛羊饲稷 - Refers to the legendary founding of the Zhou dynasty, where a cow and a sheep are said to have guided the founding ancestor to a field of millet (稷).
  • 文、行、忠、信 - Refers to the four virtues that Confucius taught: literature (文), conduct (行), loyalty (忠), and trustworthiness (信).
  • 未知生，焉知死 - A Confucian saying meaning 'If one does not know life, how can one know death?'
  • 敬鬼神而远之 - A Confucian saying meaning 'Respect the spirits and gods but keep a distance from them.'
  • 立人道之极 - Means 'to establish the highest principles of human conduct.'
  • 穷天地之变 - Means 'to explore the changes of heaven and earth.'
  • 并行而不悖 - Means 'to coexist without contradiction.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 子 - Refers to Confucius, the founder of Confucianism.
  • 左丘明 - A historian and traditional author of the *Zuo Zhuan* (Commentary of Zuo), a historical text that provides detailed accounts of the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 系词 - Refers to the *Xici* (Book of Changes), a part of the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) that discusses the principles and interpretations of the hexagrams.
  • 周易 - The *Yijing* (Book of Changes), an ancient Chinese text used for divination and philosophical reflection.
  • 诗人 - Refers to poets who wrote during the Zhou dynasty, often recording significant events and legends.

Proper Nouns

  • 子 - Confucius.
  • 左丘明 - Zuo Qiuming.
  • 系词 - *Xici* (Book of Changes).
  • 周易 - *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
  • 诗人 - Poets from the Zhou dynasty.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 余生平寡嗜好 - Means 'I have few hobbies in my lifetime,' indicating a lack of strong interests.
  • 文史外无以自娱 - Means 'outside of literature and history, I have nothing to amuse myself,' showing the author's reliance on these pursuits for enjoyment.
  • 妄言妄听 - Means 'wild talk and wild listening,' referring to unreliable or fantastical stories.
  • 记而存之 - Means 'to record and preserve them,' indicating the act of documenting these stories.
  • 非有所惑也 - Means 'not because I am deceived,' suggesting that the author is not misled by these stories.
  • 譬如嗜味者餍八珍矣 - Means 'for example, someone who enjoys flavors has had enough of the eight delicacies,' using a metaphor to illustrate a point.
  • 不广尝夫氐醢葵菹则脾困 - Means 'but if they do not widely taste such things as meat sauce and vegetable dishes, their spleen will be troubled,' continuing the metaphor.
  • 嗜音者备《咸》、《韶》矣 - Means 'someone who enjoys music has heard all of the *Xian* and *Shao*,' using another metaphor.
  • 不旁及于侏亻离亻禁亻末则耳狭 - Means 'but if they do not also listen to the strange and exotic, their ears will be narrow,' continuing the metaphor.
  • 以妄驱庸，以骇起惰 - Means 'to drive away the mediocre with the extraordinary and to rouse the lazy with the startling,' using a metaphor to describe the purpose of the stories.
  • 为之犹贤 - Means 'to do so is still better,' suggesting that engaging in these activities is preferable to inactivity.
  • 是亦裨谌适野之一乐也 - Means 'this is also one of the joys of Bichen enjoying the countryside,' using a historical reference to illustrate a point.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 余 - Refers to the author, who is likely a scholar or writer.
  • 颜鲁公 - Refers to Yan Zhenqing, a famous calligrapher and politician of the Tang dynasty.
  • 李邺侯 - Refers to Li Deyu, a prominent statesman and scholar of the Tang dynasty.
  • 韩昌黎 - Refers to Han Yu, a renowned philosopher and writer of the Tang dynasty.
  • 徐骑省 - Refers to Xu Jingzhong, a scholar and official of the Tang dynasty.
  • 裨谌 - Refers to Bi Chen, a historical figure known for his love of nature and simplicity.
  • 八珍 - Refers to the eight delicacies, a term for the finest foods.
  • 《咸》、《韶》 - Refers to the *Xian* and *Shao* musical pieces, considered the highest forms of music in ancient China.
  • 侏亻离亻禁亻末 - Refers to the strange and exotic, often used to describe unusual or foreign things.

Proper Nouns

  • 余 - The author.
  • 颜鲁公 - Yan Zhenqing.
  • 李邺侯 - Li Deyu.
  • 韩昌黎 - Han Yu.
  • 徐骑省 - Xu Jingzhong.
  • 裨谌 - Bi Chen.
  • 八珍 - The eight delicacies.
  • 《咸》、《韶》 - The *Xian* and *Shao* musical pieces.
  • 侏亻离亻禁亻末 - The strange and exotic.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 书成，初名《子不语》 - The book was completed and initially titled *The Master Didn't Speak*. *Zi Bu Yu* is a play on the name of the famous work *Zi Bu Yu* by Qing dynasty writer Ji Yun, which is a collection of strange tales.
  • 乃改为《新齐谐》云 - Changed the title to *New Qi Xie*. *Qi Xie* is another famous collection of strange tales, traditionally attributed to the ancient writer Gan Bao.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《子不语》 - A collection of strange tales by Ji Yun, a play on the name of the ancient text *Zi Bu Yu*, which is attributed to Confucius and refers to things that Confucius did not speak about, i.e., strange or supernatural phenomena.
  • 《新齐谐》 - A collection of strange tales traditionally attributed to the ancient writer Gan Bao. The title *Qi Xie* means 'strange tales' or 'odd stories'.
  • 元人说部 - Works of fiction or literature from the Yuan dynasty, known for its rich literary tradition, including novels, plays, and short stories.

Proper Nouns

  • 《子不语》 - A collection of strange tales by Ji Yun.
  • 《新齐谐》 - A collection of strange tales traditionally attributed to Gan Bao.
  • 元人说部 - Works of fiction or literature from the Yuan dynasty.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拉道士至家 - Literal meaning: 'to bring the Taoist priest to the house'; indicates an action of inviting or escorting someone.
  • 以道士语告群妾 - Literal meaning: 'to inform the group of concubines about what the Taoist priest said'; suggests a communication of information.
  • 甚喜 - Literal meaning: 'very happy'; expresses a strong positive emotion.
  • 继闻将代死也，皆恚 - Literal meaning: 'after hearing, will die in place of, all were angry'; indicates a shift in emotion from happiness to anger.
  • 各相视噤不发声 - Literal meaning: 'each looking at each other, silent and not speaking'; suggests a moment of collective silence.
  • 老仆毅然曰 - Literal meaning: 'the old servant resolutely said'; indicates a strong and determined statement.
  • 诸娘子青年可惜，老奴残年何足惜 - Literal meaning: 'the concubines are young and regrettable, the old servant's remaining years are not worth regretting'; expresses a contrast between the value of youth and the lesser value of old age.
  • 出见道士曰 - Literal meaning: 'to go out and meet the Taoist priest and say'; indicates an action of meeting and speaking.
  • 尔能无悔无怖则可 - Literal meaning: 'you can without regret or fear, then it is possible'; expresses a condition for acceptance.
  • 能 - A simple affirmative response, indicating agreement.
  • 念汝诚心 - Literal meaning: 'think of your sincere heart'; expresses an acknowledgment of sincerity.
  • 可出外与亲友作别 - Literal meaning: 'can go out to say goodbye to friends and relatives'; indicates permission to say goodbye.
  • 待我作法，三日法成，七日法验矣 - Literal meaning: 'wait for me to perform a ritual, the ritual is completed in three days, the ritual is effective in seven days'; indicates a time frame for the ritual.
  • 身至某某家，告以故，泣而诀别 - Literal meaning: 'went to a specific person's home, informed about the reason, cried and bid farewell'; indicates a visit to say goodbye.
  • 其亲友有笑者，有敬者，有怜者，有揶揄不信者 - Literal meaning: 'their friends and relatives, some laughed, some were respectful, some were sympathetic, some mocked and did not believe'; indicates varied reactions.
  • 老仆过圣帝庙——素所奉也 - Literal meaning: 'the old servant passed by the temple of the Holy Emperor, usually worshipped'; indicates a habitual act of worship.
  • 入而拜且祷曰 - Literal meaning: 'entered and worshiped and prayed and said'; indicates an act of worship and prayer.
  • 奴代家主死，求圣帝助道士放回家主魂魄 - Literal meaning: 'I will die in place of the master, request the Holy Emperor to help the Taoist priest release the master's soul back home'; indicates a request for divine assistance.
  • 语未竟，有赤脚僧立案前叱曰 - Literal meaning: 'the words were not yet finished, a barefoot monk stood in front of the table and shouted and said'; indicates an interruption.
  • 汝满面妖气，大祸至矣 - Literal meaning: 'you are covered with demonic energy, great disaster is coming'; indicates a warning.
  • 吾救汝，慎弗泄 - Literal meaning: 'I will save you, be careful not to reveal'; indicates a caution.
  • 赠一纸包曰 - Literal meaning: 'gave a paper package and said'; indicates a gift.
  • 临时取看 - Literal meaning: 'take and look when the time comes'; indicates a future action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest, indicating a religious figure in Chinese tradition.
  • 群妾 - Refers to a group of concubines, indicating a specific social status in the context of the story.
  • 老仆 - Refers to an old servant, indicating a lower social status.
  • 圣帝庙 - Refers to a temple dedicated to the Holy Emperor, indicating a place of worship.
  • 赤脚僧 - Refers to a barefoot monk, indicating a Buddhist figure.
  • 妖气 - Refers to demonic energy, indicating a supernatural element.

Proper Nouns

  • 道士 - The Taoist priest.
  • 群妾 - The group of concubines.
  • 老仆 - The old servant.
  • 圣帝庙 - The temple of the Holy Emperor.
  • 赤脚僧 - The barefoot monk.
  • 妖气 - Demonic energy.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俄而 - Temporal phrase meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly.'
  • 三日之期已屆 - Means 'the three-day period has expired' or 'the three-day deadline has arrived.'
  • 道士命移老仆床与家主灵柩相对 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the Taoist ordered the old servant's bed to be moved to face the family head's coffin' or 'the Taoist ordered the old servant to move his bed to face the family head's coffin.'
  • 铁锁扃门 - Means 'locked the door with an iron lock.'
  • 凿穴以通饮食 - Means 'dug a hole to pass food and drink.'
  • 筑坛诵咒 - Means 'built an altar and chanted spells.'
  • 居亡何 - Means 'after a while' or 'in the meantime.'
  • 了无他异 - Means 'there was no other unusual occurrence.'
  • 两黑人自地跃出 - Means 'two black people jumped out from the ground.'
  • 绿睛深目 - Means 'green eyes and deep-set eyes.'
  • 通体短毛 - Means 'covered with short fur all over the body.'
  • 长二尺许 - Means 'about two feet long.'
  • 头大如车轮 - Means 'heads as big as carriage wheels.'
  • 两夹々视老仆 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the two looked at the old servant with wide eyes' or 'the two stared at the old servant.'
  • 且视且走 - Means 'looking and walking at the same time.'
  • 绕棺而行 - Means 'walking around the coffin.'
  • 以齿啮棺缝 - Means 'biting the seams of the coffin.'
  • 缝开，闻咳嗽声，宛然家主也 - Means 'the seams opened, and a coughing sound was heard, which was clearly the family head.'
  • 二鬼启棺之前和，扶家主出 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the two demons opened the coffin lid together and helped the family head out' or 'the two demons opened the coffin lid together and supported the family head out.'
  • 状奄然若不胜病者 - Means 'looking weak and sickly.'
  • 二鬼手摩其腹，口渐有声 - Means 'the two demons rubbed their stomachs, and their mouths gradually made sounds.'
  • 形是家主，音则道士 - Means 'the appearance was that of the family head, but the voice was that of the Taoist.'
  • 愀然曰 - Means 'said with a worried expression.'
  • 圣帝之言，得无验乎 - Means 'the words of the Holy Emperor, could they not be true?'
  • 急揣怀中纸 - Means 'quickly took out the paper from his pocket.'
  • 五爪飞出，变为金龙，长数丈 - Means 'five claws flew out and transformed into a golden dragon, several zhang long.'
  • 攫老仆于室中，以绳缚梁上 - Means 'seized the old servant in the room and tied him to the beam with a rope.'
  • 老仆昏然 - Means 'the old servant became dazed.'
  • 注目下视 - Means 'looked down with his eyes.'
  • 二鬼扶家主自棺中出，至老仆卧床，无人焉者 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the two demons helped the family head out of the coffin and reached the old servant's bed, but there was no one there' or 'the two demons helped the family head out of the coffin and arrived at the old servant's bed, but there was no one there.'
  • 家主大呼曰 - Means 'the family head shouted loudly.'
  • 法败矣 - Means 'the method has failed.'
  • 二鬼狰狞 - Means 'the two demons looked ferocious.'
  • 绕屋寻觅，卒不得 - Means 'searched around the house but finally could not find.'
  • 家主怒甚，取老仆床帐被褥，碎裂之 - Means 'the family head was very angry and tore up the old servant's bed curtains and quilts.'
  • 一鬼仰头，见老仆在梁，大喜，与家主腾身取之 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'one demon looked up, saw the old servant on the beam, and was very happy, then jumped up with the family head to catch him' or 'one demon looked up, saw the old servant on the beam, and was very happy, then jumped up with the family head to grab him.'
  • 未及屋梁，震雷一声，仆坠于地，棺合如故，二鬼亦不复见矣 - Means 'before reaching the roof beam, a thunderclap sounded, and the old servant fell to the ground, the coffin closed as before, and the two demons disappeared again.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or practitioner.
  • 家主 - Refers to the head of the family or the deceased.
  • 圣帝 - Refers to a holy emperor or a deity in Taoist belief.
  • 棺 - Refers to a coffin.
  • 金龙 - Refers to a golden dragon, often symbolizing power and protection in Chinese mythology.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 道士命移老仆床与家主灵柩相对 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the Taoist ordered the old servant's bed to be moved to face the family head's coffin' or 'the Taoist ordered the old servant to move his bed to face the family head's coffin.'
  • 二鬼启棺之前和，扶家主出 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the two demons opened the coffin lid together and helped the family head out' or 'the two demons opened the coffin lid together and supported the family head out.'
  • 二鬼扶家主自棺中出，至老仆卧床，无人焉者 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'the two demons helped the family head out of the coffin and reached the old servant's bed, but there was no one there' or 'the two demons helped the family head out of the coffin and arrived at the old servant's bed, but there was no one there.'
  • 一鬼仰头，见老仆在梁，大喜，与家主腾身取之 - Ambiguous phrase. Could mean 'one demon looked up, saw the old servant on the beam, and was very happy, then jumped up with the family head to catch him' or 'one demon looked up, saw the old servant on the beam, and was very happy, then jumped up with the family head to grab him.'

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 具道所见 - To detail what was seen.
  • 结绳于梁 - Tied a rope to the beam.
  • 伏地再拜 - Prostrated herself and bowed again.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Refers to being struck dead by thunder, a common motif in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • Refers to the practice of alchemy and transformation, often associated with Taoist sorcery.
  • Refers to the pursuit of wealth and lust, often used to describe the sins of the wicked.
  • Refers to the strictest penalties prescribed by heavenly law, often used in legal and religious contexts.
  • Refers to the northern gate of Hangzhou, a significant location in the city.
  • Refers to the frequent appearance of ghosts, a common theme in Chinese ghost stories.
  • Refers to a high-ranking official, often a governor or provincial administrator.

Proper Nouns

  • 北关门外 - The northern gate of Hangzhou.
  • 蔡姓者 - Piao, a historical figure.
  • 蔡炳侯方伯 - Piao Binghou, the Governor.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 披闼 - Literally means 'to push open a door,' but in this context, it implies the ghost entering the room.
  • 登床抚其背 - Means 'to climb onto the bed and pat his back,' a gesture of familiarity and comfort.
  • 暴疾亡 - Means 'sudden illness and death,' emphasizing the abrupt nature of the event.
  • 特来诀别 - Means 'especially to come and say goodbye,' indicating a final farewell.
  • 身后相托 - Means 'to entrust after death,' referring to the ghost's request for assistance after his passing.
  • 欠卖笔者钱数千 - Means 'owing the scribe several thousand coins,' indicating a financial debt.
  • 镌刻 - Means 'to engrave or print,' referring to the act of publishing the deceased's writings.
  • 微名不泯 - Means 'to ensure the faint name does not fade,' expressing the desire for the deceased's legacy to be remembered.
  • 偿之 - Means 'to repay,' referring to settling the debt.
  • 言毕欲走 - Means 'having finished speaking, he wanted to leave,' indicating the ghost's intention to depart.
  • 渐无怖意 - Means 'gradually losing fear,' describing the younger man's changing emotional state.
  • 长诀 - Means 'a long farewell,' indicating a permanent separation.
  • 稍缓须叟 - Means 'to delay a little while,' suggesting a request for more time.
  • 回坐其床 - Means 'to sit back on the bed,' indicating a return to a previous position.
  • 更叙平生 - Means 'to recount life events,' describing the act of sharing personal history.
  • 言毕 - Means 'having finished speaking,' indicating the end of a conversation.
  • 欲走 - Means 'to want to leave,' indicating the ghost's intention to depart.
  • 近人情 - Means 'close to human nature,' describing the ghost's actions as relatable.
  • 貌如平昔 - Means 'looking as he did before,' indicating no change in appearance.
  • 心稍定 - Means 'the heart gradually calmed,' describing the younger man's emotional state.
  • 唯唯 - An interjection indicating agreement or assent.
  • 泣留之 - Means 'to cry and try to keep him,' indicating an attempt to persuade the ghost to stay.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 立而不行 - Standing but not moving, describing the corpse's unnatural behavior.
  • 两眼瞠视 - With wide-open eyes staring, depicting the corpse's expressionless and eerie gaze.
  • 貌渐丑败 - Appearance gradually becoming ugly and decayed, describing the corpse's deteriorating state.
  • 少者惧 - The young person is frightened, indicating the emotional reaction of the protagonist.
  • 促之曰 - Urging him to say, referring to the young person's attempt to make the corpse leave.
  • 君言既毕 - Your words have been spoken, a polite way of telling the corpse to depart.
  • 可去矣 - You may leave now, a formal command to the corpse.
  • 尸竟不去 - The corpse still does not leave, emphasizing the persistence of the supernatural event.
  • 拍床大呼 - Slapping the bed and shouting loudly, describing the young person's desperate action.
  • 屹立如故 - Standing firmly as before, indicating the corpse's unyielding presence.
  • 愈骇 - Even more frightened, showing the increasing terror of the protagonist.
  • 奔 - To run, describing the chase between the young person and the corpse.
  • 逾墙仆地 - Jump over the wall and fall to the ground, depicting the young person's desperate escape attempt.
  • 垂首墙外 - Hanging his head over the wall, describing the corpse's inability to follow the young person.
  • 口中涎沫与少者之面相滴涔涔也 - Saliva dripping from the mouth onto the young person's face, a vivid description of the corpse's proximity.
  • 天明 - At dawn, marking the transition from night to day.
  • 路人过之 - Passersby passing by, indicating the arrival of witnesses.
  • 饮以姜汁 - Giving him ginger juice to drink, a traditional remedy for shock or illness.
  • 少者苏 - The young person regains consciousness, signaling the end of the supernatural event.
  • 尸主家方觅见不得 - The family of the corpse was searching for it but had not found it, showing the prior disappearance of the corpse.
  • 舁归成殡 - Carried back to be buried, describing the final disposition of the corpse.
  • 识者曰 - Those who understand say, introducing a commentary on the event.
  • 人之魂善而魄恶 - The soul is good while the ghostly essence is evil, a philosophical distinction between soul and ghostly essence.
  • 人之魂灵而魄愚 - The soul is intelligent while the ghostly essence is foolish, further elaborating on the nature of soul and ghostly essence.
  • 一灵不泯 - The soul remains intact, describing the initial state of the corpse.
  • 魂一散而魄滞 - When the soul disperses, the ghostly essence lingers, explaining the behavior of the corpse after the soul leaves.
  • 世之移尸走影 - The movement of corpses and shadows in the world, referring to similar supernatural phenomena.
  • 皆魄为之 - All are caused by the ghostly essence, attributing such phenomena to the ghostly essence.
  • 惟有道之人为能制魄 - Only those with virtue can control the ghostly essence, suggesting a moral or spiritual solution to such phenomena.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 曾虚舟 - A historical figure known for his enigmatic behavior and prophetic abilities.
  • 佯狂 - A traditional Chinese practice of feigning madness to avoid political persecution or to gain attention.
  • 吴、楚间 - Refers to the regions of Wu and Chu, which were significant during the Three Kingdoms period and later dynasties.
  • 言多奇中 - Reflects the belief in the power of oracles and seers in Chinese culture.
  • 所到处 - Wherever he went, indicating the widespread influence of the individual.
  • 老幼男妇环之而行 - Elders, young, men, and women surrounded him as he walked, describing the crowd that followed the individual.
  • 所言辄中人隐 - What he said always hit the mark in people's hidden thoughts, emphasizing the individual's insight.
  • 或与人好言 - Sometimes speaking kindly to someone, showing the individual's ability to manipulate emotions.
  • 人大哭去 - The person cried and left, indicating the emotional impact of the individual's words.
  • 或笞骂人 - Sometimes scolding or cursing someone, showing the individual's ability to provoke reactions.
  • 人大喜过望 - The person was overjoyed, indicating the unexpected positive effect of the individual's words.
  • 在问者自知之 - Those who asked knew it themselves, suggesting that the individual's words revealed truths to the inquirer.
  • 旁人不知 - Others did not know, indicating the selective nature of the individual's influence.

Proper Nouns

  • 曾虚舟 - A historical figure known for his enigmatic behavior and prophetic abilities.
  • 吴、楚间 - Refers to the regions of Wu and Chu, which were significant during the Three Kingdoms period and later dynasties.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 走问之 - Hastened to ask him; indicates urgency and eagerness.
  • 子坚悚然而归 - Wang Zijian was startled and returned home; indicates a strong emotional reaction.
  • 吾死矣 - I am going to die; indicates a sense of impending doom.
  • 吾梦见二隶人从地下耸身起，至榻前拉吾同行 - I dreamed that two servants emerged from the ground, came to my bed, and pulled me along; indicates a vivid and dramatic dream.
  • 路泱泱然，黄沙白草，了不见人 - The road was vast, with yellow sand and white grass, and no one was seen; describes a desolate and eerie scene.
  • 行数里，引入一官衙，有神乌纱冠，南向坐 - After walking several miles, they led me to a government office, where a god wearing a black cap sat facing south; describes a supernatural encounter.
  • 隶掖我跪堂下 - The servants pushed me to kneel in front of the hall; indicates a submissive position.
  • 汝知罪乎？ - Do you know your sins?; indicates a questioning of one's actions.
  • 罪小。 - The sin is small.; indicates a judgment of the severity of a sin.
  • 好讥弹人文章。 - I have spoken ill of others' writings.; indicates a confession of a specific sin.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 风水 - Feng shui, a traditional Chinese practice of arranging buildings and landscapes to harmonize with the surrounding environment.
  • 虚舟 - Xu Zhou, a person known for his wisdom and possibly supernatural abilities.
  • 御史 - A censor, an official responsible for supervising government officials and maintaining moral standards.
  • 孝廉 - A title given to those who passed the imperial examinations and were recommended for government service.
  • 乌纱冠 - A black cap, a symbol of authority in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 杭州 - The city of Hangzhou.
  • 泸溪县 - Luxi County.
  • 虚舟 - Xu Zhou.
  • 御史 - Censor.
  • 钟孝廉 - Zhong Shoulian.
  • 常熟 - Changshu.
  • 孝廉 - Title for those who passed the imperial examinations.
  • 乌纱冠 - Black cap.

[10]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 此更小矣 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'this is even smaller' or 'this is even worse.'
  • 然则某无他罪 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'then I have no other sins.'
  • 令渠照来 - Command or instruction meaning 'let him show himself' or 'make him appear.'
  • 恍惚悟前生姓杨，名敝 - Vivid expression meaning 'he vaguely remembered his past life, his name was Yang Bi.'
  • 不觉战栗 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he couldn't help but tremble.'
  • 匐伏神前 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he prostrated himself before the deity.'
  • 知罪 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'I know my guilt.'
  • 还不变么 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'won't you change?'
  • 举手拍案，霹雳一声 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he raised his hand and struck the table, there was a thunderclap.'
  • 天崩地坼 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'the sky and earth split apart.'
  • 城郭、衙署、神鬼、器械之类，了无所睹 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'the city walls, government offices, gods, ghosts, and weapons, all vanished.'
  • 但见汪洋大水，无边无岸 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'all he saw was vast, boundless water.'
  • 一身渺然，飘浮于菜叶之上 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he felt small and insignificant, floating on a piece of lettuce.'
  • 自念叶轻身重，何得不坠 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he thought the leaf was light and his body heavy, how could he not sink?'
  • 回视己身，已化蛆虫 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he looked at himself and saw he had turned into a maggot.'
  • 耳目口鼻，悉如芥子 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'his eyes, ears, nose, and mouth were all as small as mustard seeds.'
  • 不觉大哭而醒 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'he couldn't help but cry and woke up.'
  • 吾梦若是，其能久乎 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'if my dream is like this, how long can it last?'
  • 先生毋苦，梦不足凭也 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'Mr., don't be sad, dreams are not to be trusted.'
  • 先生命速具棺殓之物 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'the man ordered the preparation of a coffin and burial items.'
  • 越三日，呕血暴亡 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'three days later, he vomited blood and died suddenly.'

  • 汪洋大水 - Refers to vast, boundless water, often used in Chinese literature to symbolize chaos or the unknown.
  • 菜叶 - Refers to a piece of lettuce, a common vegetable in Chinese cuisine.
  • 蛆虫 - Refers to maggots, often used in Chinese literature to symbolize decay or transformation.
  • 芥子 - Refers to mustard seeds, often used in Chinese literature to symbolize smallness or insignificance.
  • 棺殓之物 - Refers to the items needed for a burial, including a coffin and other funeral supplies.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公下階迎，揖以上坐 - Formal expression describing the host's welcoming gesture, where '下階迎' means 'descended the steps to greet' and '揖以上坐' means 'bowed and invited to sit above.'

  • 公來何為 - Question meaning 'Why did you come?' with '何為' being a formal way of asking 'for what purpose.'

  • 朝廷國課，尚苦不輸 - Reference to the difficulty in paying taxes to the imperial court, where '國課' means 'imperial tax' and '苦不輸' means 'hardly able to pay.'

  • 诱人修斋打醮，倾家者不下千万 - Describes the practice of luring people into religious rituals, where '诱人修斋打醮' means 'luring people into fasting and rituals,' and '倾家者不下千万' means 'not less than ten thousand families have been ruined.'
  • 鬼神幽明道隔，不能家喻户晓 - Refers to the difficulty in making the distinction between ghosts and spirits known to everyone, with '幽明道隔' meaning 'separated by a path' and '不能家喻户晓' meaning 'cannot be known to every household.'
  • 明公为民除弊，虽不来此，谁敢相违 - Praises the official for removing corruption, where '除弊' means 'remove corruption' and '谁敢相违' means 'who would dare to disobey.'
  • 今更宠临，具征仁勇 - Commends the official's visit, where '宠临' means 'graceful visit' and '具征仁勇' means 'fully demonstrates benevolence and courage.'
  • 伏魔大帝至矣，公少避 - Indicates the arrival of the Emperor of Subduing Demons, where '伏魔大帝' means 'Emperor of Subduing Demons' and '少避' means 'please step aside.'
  • 关神绿袍长髯，冉冉而下 - Describes the appearance of Guan Yu, where '绿袍长髯' means 'green robe and long beard' and '冉冉而下' means 'slowly descended.'
  • 与包公行宾主礼 - Describes the formal greeting, where '行宾主礼' means 'observed the formal guest-host etiquette.'
  • 语多不可辨 - Indicates that much of the conversation was incomprehensible, with '语多不可辨' meaning 'much of the conversation was indistinguishable.'
  • 公处有生人气，何也 - Question about the presence of a living person's energy, where '生人气' means 'living person's energy' and '何也' means 'why?'
  • 贤令也，我愿见之 - Expression of admiration, where '贤令' means 'excellent magistrate' and '我愿见之' means 'I wish to see him.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces a prediction and its fulfillment. The prediction is that the imbalance of yin and yang will lead to a disaster, and it later turns out to be true.
  • This sentence describes a scene where the governor sees a woman at night. The sentence structure is complex, with multiple descriptive elements.
  • This sentence describes the interaction between the governor and the ghost. The governor recognizes the woman as a ghost and commands her to enter. The ghost gradually enters and speaks in a hoarse voice.
  • This sentence explains the ghost's story. She says she is a widow who remained loyal to her husband and was forced into a marriage by her husband's brother, which led to her suicide.
  • This sentence describes the investigation and punishment of the husband's brother. The brother initially denies the charges but is convinced by the ghost's appearance and confesses. He is then punished, and the entire county is amazed.
  • This sentence describes the governor's actions and the criticism he receives. He writes a stele inscription to honor the woman, but the Sichuan governor criticizes him for relying on ghosts and spirits. The governor feels ashamed but acknowledges that the story is mostly true and could not be kept secret.

  • 呼庙僧问曰 - Means 'he called the temple monk and asked.'
  • 汝里中得毋有万蓝乎 - Means 'is there no Wan Lan in your village?'

  • 唤蓝至，问 - Means 'he called Wan Lan and asked.'
  • 尔屋祖遗乎 - Means 'is your house inherited from your ancestors?'
  • 非也 - Means 'no.'
  • 此屋本从前观音堂大门出入之地 - Means 'this house was originally the entrance and exit of the Guanyin Hall's main gate.'
  • 今年正月，寺僧盗售于我，价二十金 - Means 'last January, the temple monk sold it to me for twenty gold pieces.'
  • 赵亦不告以梦，即二十金为赎还基址，加修葺焉 - Means 'Zhao did not tell him about the dream, but paid twenty gold pieces to redeem the land and repair it.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是时 - Indicates 'at this time' or 'during this period.'
  • 数月后 - Means 'several months later.'
  • 夫人有身 - Means 'the lady became pregnant.' In Classical Chinese, '有身' is an idiomatic expression for being pregnant.
  • 将产之夕 - Means 'on the night before she was to give birth.'
  • 梦老妪复来 - Means 'dreamed of an old woman coming again.' '复来' means 'coming again' or 'returning.'
  • 抱一儿与之 - Means 'holding a child and giving it to her.'
  • 遂产一儿 - Means 'she then gave birth to a son.' '遂' means 'then' or 'therefore.'
  • 阅邸抄 - Means 'read the official gazette.' '邸抄' refers to the official news bulletin.
  • 见景山遗失陈设古玩数件 - Means 'saw that several pieces of antique decorations had been lost at Jing Mountain.' '陈设' means 'decorations' or 'displays.'
  • 内务府官疑挑土工人所窃 - Means 'the officials of the Imperial Household Department suspected that the workers digging soil had stolen them.' '疑' means 'suspected.'
  • 召执役者数十人，立而讯之 - Means 'summoned dozens of laborers and interrogated them standing.' '执役者' means 'laborers' or 'workers.'
  • 一人忽跪诉曰 - Means 'one person suddenly knelt and said.' '忽' means 'suddenly.'
  • 我常格也，系正黄旗人，年十二岁 - Means 'I am Changge, a member of the Plain Yellow Banner, aged twelve.' '系' means 'is' or 'belongs to.'
  • 赴市买物，为工人赵二图奸不遂，将刀杀死，埋我于厚载门外堆炭地方 - Means 'I went to the market to buy things, and was about to be raped by worker Zhao Er but failed, so he killed me with a knife and buried me outside the Thick Bearing Gate in a pile of coal.' '图奸' means 'to attempt rape,' and '不遂' means 'failed.'
  • 我家父母某，尚未知也 - Means 'my parents, whose names I will not mention, do not yet know.' '某' is used to avoid mentioning specific names.
  • 求大人掘验伸冤 - Means 'I beg you to dig and verify my innocence.' '伸冤' means 'to clear one's name.'
  • 言毕仆地 - Means 'after speaking, he fell to the ground.' '言毕' means 'after speaking,' and '仆地' means 'fell to the ground.'
  • 少顷，复跃而起曰 - Means 'a short while later, he jumped up and said.' '少顷' means 'a short while later,' and '复跃而起' means 'jumped up again.'
  • 我即赵二，杀常格者我也 - Means 'I am Zhao Er, the one who killed Changge.' '杀常格者' means 'the killer of Changge.'
  • 内务府大人见其状，知有冤 - Means 'the official of the Imperial Household Department saw his state and knew there was injustice.' '见其状' means 'saw his state,' and '知有冤' means 'knew there was injustice.'
  • 移交刑部掘验 - Means 'transferred the case to the Ministry of Punishment for digging and verification.' '移交' means 'transferred,' and '掘验' means 'digging and verification.'
  • 尸伤宛然 - Means 'the injuries on the body were clearly visible.' '宛然' means 'clearly visible.'
  • 访其父母 - Means 'visited his parents.' '访' means 'visited.'
  • 尽吐情实 - Means 'fully confessed the truth.' '尽吐' means 'fully confessed,' and '情实' means 'truth.'
  • 刑部奏 - Means 'the Ministry of Punishment reported.' '奏' means 'reported.'
  • 赵二自吐凶情，迹似自首，例宜减等 - Means 'Zhao Er confessed the crime himself, which seems like self-reporting, and according to the law, he should receive a reduced sentence.' '自吐凶情' means 'confessed the crime himself,' '迹似自首' means 'seems like self-reporting,' and '例宜减等' means 'according to the law, he should receive a reduced sentence.'
  • 但为冤鬼所凭，不便援引此例 - Means 'but because he was possessed by a vengeful spirit, it is inconvenient to apply this rule.' '为冤鬼所凭' means 'possessed by a vengeful spirit,' and '不便援引此例' means 'it is inconvenient to apply this rule.'
  • 拟斩立决 - Means 'proposed immediate execution.' '拟' means 'proposed,' and '斩立决' means 'immediate execution.'
  • 奉旨依议 - Means 'the imperial decree followed the advice.' '奉旨' means 'the imperial decree,' and '依议' means 'followed the advice.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妻坚拒，泣曰 - 坚拒 means 'firmly refused,' 泣曰 means 'cried and said.'
  • 吾某村中王姑娘也 - 吾 means 'I,' 某村中 means 'in a certain village,' 王姑娘 means 'Miss Wang.'
  • 尚未婚嫁，何为至此 - 尚未婚嫁 means 'not yet married,' 何为至此 means 'why am I here?'
  • 吾之父母姊妹，俱在何处 - 吾之父母姊妹 means 'my parents and sisters,' 俱在何处 means 'where are they all?'
  • 其夫大骇，急告某村 - 大骇 means 'greatly frightened,' 急告 means 'hurriedly informed.'

  • 其父母狂奔而至 - 狂奔 means 'rushed wildly,' 而至 means 'and arrived.'

  • 事皆符合 - 事皆符合 means 'all the facts matched.'
  • 其未婚之家亦来视 - 未婚之家 means 'her family who had not yet married her,' 亦来视 means 'also came to look at her.'
  • 妇犹羞涩，赤见于面 - 犹羞涩 means 'still shy,' 赤见于面 means 'her face turned red.'
  • 遂两家争此妇，鸣于官 - 遂 means 'then,' 两家争此妇 means 'the two families disputed over this woman,' 鸣于官 means 'appealed to the authorities.'
  • 砚庭为之作合，断归村农 - 砚庭 refers to a judge named Yan Ting, 为之作合 means 'arranged the marriage,' 断归村农 means 'ruled that she should go to the village farmer.'
  • 乾隆二十一年事 - 乾隆二十一年 means 'in the 21st year of the Qianlong era,' 事 means 'the matter' or 'the event.'

  • 遲至千年 - Delayed until a thousand years later.
  • 蓋張以忠節故 - Because Zhang was loyal and righteous.
  • 而報復難 - And it is difficult to avenge.

  • 家人未敢棺殓 - The family dared not coffin and wrap the body.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 殿前有黄金扁额 - Refers to a golden plaque in front of the palace, likely indicating a formal or ceremonial setting.
  • 书‘地穷宫’三字 - The inscription '地穷宫' (Dì Qióng Gōng) is likely a symbolic name for the location, possibly indicating a place at the edge of the known world or a remote, mysterious place.
  • 我玩视良久 - Literally 'I gazed at it for a long time,' indicating prolonged observation or contemplation.
  • 袍带者怒 - 'Pao Dai' (袍带) refers to officials or courtiers, often dressed in formal robes and sashes. '怒' means 'angry,' suggesting the official's reaction to the narrator's presence.
  • 来逐我曰 - '逐' means 'to chase away' or 'to expel,' indicating the official's attempt to remove the narrator.
  • 此何地，容尔立耶？ - A rhetorical question meaning 'What place is this? Can you stand here?' The official is questioning the narrator's right to be there.
  • 我素刚，不肯去 - '素刚' means 'usually stubborn' or 'always firm,' indicating the narrator's refusal to leave.
  • 与之争 - '争' means 'to argue' or 'to dispute,' suggesting a confrontation between the narrator and the official.
  • 殿内传呼曰 - '传呼' means 'to announce' or 'to call out,' indicating that someone inside the palace is making an announcement.
  • 外何喧嚷？ - '喧嚷' means 'uproar' or 'commotion,' referring to the noise outside the palace.
  • 袍带者入，良久出曰 - '袍带者' refers to the official, who enters the palace and returns after a long time.
  • 汝毋去，听候谕旨 - '毋去' means 'do not leave,' and '听候谕旨' means 'await the imperial decree,' indicating that the official is instructing the narrator to stay and wait for further orders.
  • 二人环而守之 - '环而守之' means 'to surround and guard,' indicating that two people are watching over the narrator.
  • 天渐暮，阴风四起，霜片如瓦 - Describes the weather: '天渐暮' means 'the sky gradually darkens,' '阴风四起' means 'gale winds blow from all sides,' and '霜片如瓦' means 'frost flakes as thick as tiles,' creating a vivid image of a cold, stormy night.
  • 我冻久战栗 - '冻久' means 'frozen for a long time,' and '战栗' means 'shivering,' describing the narrator's physical state.
  • 两守者亦瑟缩流涕，指我怨曰 - '瑟缩' means 'trembling,' '流涕' means 'tears streaming,' and '指我怨曰' means 'pointing at me and complaining,' indicating that the guards are also cold and upset.
  • 微汝来作闹，我辈岂受此冷夜之苦哉！ - A rhetorical question meaning 'If it weren't for you causing trouble, we wouldn't be suffering through this cold night!' The guards are blaming the narrator for their discomfort.
  • 天稍明，殿内钟动，风霜亦霁 - '天稍明' means 'the sky begins to brighten,' '钟动' means 'the bell rings,' and '风霜亦霁' means 'the wind and frost also clear up,' describing the transition from night to morning.
  • 又一人出曰 - '又一人出' means 'another person comes out,' indicating that a new figure appears.
  • 昨所留人，著送归本处 - '昨所留人' means 'the person left yesterday,' and '著送归本处' means 'order him to be sent back to his original place,' indicating that the narrator is being ordered to return home.
  • 袍带者拉以行 - '拉以行' means 'to pull and lead,' indicating that the official is leading the narrator away.
  • 仍过原处，见牧羊人尚在 - '仍过原处' means 'return to the original place,' and '见牧羊人尚在' means 'see the shepherd still there,' indicating that the narrator returns to the same location where the shepherd is waiting.
  • 袍带者以我授之曰 - '以我授之' means 'hand me over to him,' indicating that the official is passing the narrator to the shepherd.
  • 奉旨交此人与汝，送他还家，我去矣 - '奉旨' means 'by imperial order,' '交此人与汝' means 'hand this person over to you,' and '送他还家' means 'send him back home,' indicating that the official is passing the narrator to the shepherd by imperial command.
  • 牧羊人殴我以拳 - '殴我以拳' means 'hit me with his fists,' describing the shepherd's violent action.
  • 惧而坠河，饮水腹胀，一溺遂苏 - '惧而坠河' means 'out of fear, I fell into the river,' '饮水腹胀' means 'drank water and my stomach swelled,' and '一溺遂苏' means 'after one bout of urination, I revived,' describing the narrator's near-drowning experience and recovery.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 殿前有黄金扁额 - The presence of a golden plaque in front of the palace suggests a formal or ceremonial setting, possibly a government office or a temple.
  • 书‘地穷宫’三字 - The inscription '地穷宫' (Dì Qióng Gōng) is likely a symbolic name for the location, possibly indicating a place at the edge of the known world or a remote, mysterious place.
  • 袍带者 - Refers to officials or courtiers, often dressed in formal robes and sashes, indicating a high-status setting.
  • 传呼 - The act of announcing or calling out is a common practice in official settings, often used to summon people or make announcements.
  • 听候谕旨 - Waiting for the imperial decree is a common practice in imperial China, where decisions were often made by the emperor and communicated through officials.
  • 阴风四起，霜片如瓦 - The description of the weather is vivid and evocative, suggesting a harsh and unforgiving environment, possibly a metaphor for the narrator's difficult situation.
  • 微汝来作闹，我辈岂受此冷夜之苦哉！ - The guards' complaint reflects the social hierarchy and the common practice of blaming lower-status individuals for one's own discomfort.
  • 天稍明，殿内钟动，风霜亦霁 - The transition from night to morning and the clearing of the weather suggest a change in circumstances, possibly indicating a resolution or a new phase in the story.
  • 奉旨交此人与汝，送他还家，我去矣 - The official's order by imperial command reflects the centralized authority of the imperial system, where even minor decisions could be made by the emperor and carried out by officials.

Idiomatic Expressions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 炼丹道士 - Refers to a Daoist alchemist who practices alchemy to produce elixirs of immortality.
  • 楚中大宗伯 - Refers to Zhang Luhao, a high-ranking official (大宗伯) from the Chu region. *大宗伯* is a title from the Zhou dynasty, equivalent to a modern-day minister of rites or culture.
  • 好道 - Means 'loves the Dao' or 'is devoted to Daoist practices.'
  • 予告归 - Means 'I was granted leave to return home,' indicating the author was on official leave.
  • 寄居江宁 - Means 'lodged temporarily in Jiangning,' referring to the author's temporary residence in Jiangning (modern-day Nanjing).
  • 拥朱提一百六十万 - Refers to having 1.6 million *zhūtí* (朱提), an ancient unit of silver. *朱提* is a type of high-quality silver coin from the Western Han dynasty.
  • 黄白之术 - Refers to alchemical techniques for transmuting base metals into gold and silver.
  • 寿九百余岁 - Means 'lives for over nine hundred years,' a hyperbolic claim about the longevity of the Daoist.
  • 烧杏核成银 - Means 'burns almond kernels into silver,' referring to a purported alchemical demonstration.
  • 屡试若神 - Means 'tested repeatedly with divine results,' indicating the Daoist's success in alchemical demonstrations.
  • 斋戒三日 - Means 'fasted for three days,' a ritual purification before alchemical work.
  • 定坎离之位 - Refers to setting the positions of *Kan* (坎, water) and *Li* (离, fire) in alchemical symbolism. *Kan* and *Li* are trigrams in the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) associated with water and fire, respectively, and are central to alchemical theory.
  • 每一炉，辄下银五万两，炭百担 - Means 'for each furnace, five million *liang* of silver and one hundred loads of charcoal were used.' *liang* (两) is an ancient unit of weight, and *dan* (担) is a unit of weight or measure.
  • 昼则公亲监之，夜则使人守之 - Means 'during the day, the official personally supervised the process, and at night, he had someone guard it.'
  • 银登时化为水 - Means 'the silver instantly turned into water,' referring to the alchemical process.
  • 满百万则丹成 - Means 'once one million is reached, the elixir will be completed,' indicating the required amount of silver for the alchemical process.
  • 含之：不饥不寒，可南可北，随意所之，无不可到 - Means 'if one consumes it, they will not feel hunger or cold, and can go south or north, wherever they wish, and there is nowhere they cannot reach,' describing the supposed effects of the elixir.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 炼丹 - Refers to alchemy, a practice that combines elements of Daoist philosophy, medicine, and chemistry. The goal of alchemy was to produce an elixir of immortality.
  • 楚中 - Refers to the Chu region, a historical area in southern China. The term *楚* is often associated with Daoist traditions and alchemy.
  • 大宗伯 - A high-ranking official title from the Zhou dynasty, equivalent to a modern-day minister of rites or culture. Zhang Luhao held this title, indicating his high social and political status.
  • 朱提 - An ancient Chinese silver coin from the Western Han dynasty, known for its high quality and value.
  • 黄白之术 - Alchemy, specifically the practice of transmuting base metals into gold and silver. This was a common theme in Daoist texts and alchemical literature.
  • 坎离 - Two of the eight trigrams in the *Yijing* (Book of Changes). *Kan* (坎) represents water, and *Li* (离) represents fire. In alchemical theory, they symbolize the balance of opposing forces necessary for the creation of the elixir.
  • 坎离之位 - Refers to the arrangement of *Kan* and *Li* in alchemical processes, which is believed to be essential for the success of the alchemical work.

Proper Nouns

  • 张履昊 - Zhang Luhao, the high-ranking official and Daoist alchemist.
  • 楚中 - The Chu region, a historical area in southern China.
  • 朱提 - An ancient Chinese silver coin from the Western Han dynasty.
  • 黄白之术 - Alchemy, specifically the practice of transmuting base metals into gold and silver.
  • 坎离 - Two of the eight trigrams in the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), representing water and fire, respectively.
  • 郎总兵 - A military official who recommended the Daoist to Zhang Luhao.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 至三鼓，有人撞门，声甚厉 - This clause describes the timing and intensity of the knock, which is crucial for setting the scene.
  • 顾喝问：“何人？”外应曰：“沈定兰也。” - This exchange introduces the character of Shen Dinglan and sets up the tension.
  • 顾大怖，不肯开 - This clause shows the protagonist's fear and reluctance to open the door.
  • 门外大呼曰：“尔无怖，我有事托君。” - This clause reveals the ghost's intention and the urgency of the situation.
  • 所以唤尔开门者，正以照常行事，存故人之情耳 - This clause explains the ghost's reasoning and the importance of maintaining old friendships.
  • 顾不得已为启其钥，砉然有声，如人坠地 - This clause describes the protagonist's actions and the unexpected result.
  • 顾手忙眼颤，意欲举烛 - This clause captures the protagonist's state of panic and the desire to light a candle.
  • 忽地上又大呼曰：“我非沈定兰也。我乃东家新死李某，被奸妇毒死，故托名沈定兰，求汝伸冤。” - This clause reveals the true identity of the ghost and the reason for the appeal.
  • 我非官府，冤何能伸？ - This clause expresses the protagonist's helplessness and the impossibility of redressing the injustice.
  • 尸伤可验 - This clause suggests a way to verify the ghost's claims.
  • 灯至即见。但见灯，我便不能言矣 - This clause sets up a condition for further communication and the ghost's limitations.

  • 沈定兰 - The name of the deceased friend.
  • 李某 - The name of the recently deceased man.
  • 奸妇 - Refers to the woman who poisoned Li.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥 - This opening simile governs the whole sentence and frames writing through the logic of horsemanship.
  • 雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之 - The concessive pattern shows that natural brilliance is acknowledged but must still be restrained.
  • 勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也 - The final prohibition can be read as two linked dangers: straying from the proper track and letting the writing run off into structural disorder.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '方惊疑问，有箍桶匠扶之而归' is elliptical and must be read as a single event where the person is startled and questioned, and then a barrel maker helps him back home.
  • The sequence of actions '灌以姜汁，涂以朱砂，始作声' must be read as a single event where the person is given ginger juice and smeared with cinnabar, and then starts to speak.
  • The sequence of events '我初出门，街上有黑衣人向我拱手，我便昏迷，随之而行' must be read as a single narrative where the person goes out, encounters a black-clothed person, becomes unconscious, and follows them.
  • The direct speech '其人云：“你到家收拾行李，与我同游水仙殿，何如？”' must be read as a single statement from the black-clothed person.
  • The direct speech '忽见白头翁在后喝曰：“恶鬼迷人，勿往！勿往！”' must be read as a single statement from the old man with white hair.
  • The sentence '谛视之，乃亡父也' must be read as a single realization where the person recognizes the old man as his deceased father.
  • The sentence '黑衣人遂与亡父互相欧击' must be read as a single event where the black-clothed person fights with the deceased father.
  • The sentence '适箍桶匠走来，如有热风吹入水中者' must be read as a single event where the barrel maker arrives, as if a hot wind blew into the water.

  • 两神相殴 - Literally 'two gods fighting,' indicating a supernatural confrontation.
  • 孝廉 - A title of honor and a system of recommendation used in the Han dynasty for selecting officials based on moral character and virtue.
  • 衣食不周 - Means 'not having enough clothing or food,' indicating poverty.
  • 意郁郁不乐 - A compound phrase meaning 'his mind was depressed and unhappy.'
  • 病临危 - Refers to being on the verge of death, literally 'when the illness reached its critical stage.'
  • 慎毋置我棺中 - 'Be careful not to put me in the coffin,' a direct and unusual request.
  • 有不平事，将诉冥王 - 'I have an injustice to report to the King of Hell.'
  • 或有灵应，亦未可知 - 'Perhaps there will be a supernatural response, but I cannot say for sure.'
  • 中心尚温 - 'The center (of the body) was still warm,' indicating recent death.
  • 横尸以待 - 'Lay the body horizontally and wait,' suggesting a waiting period for a supernatural event.
  • 果苏 - 'Indeed, he awoke,' indicating that the person revived.
  • 所见人民往来，与阳世一般 - 'The people coming and going were the same as in the world of the living,' suggesting the afterlife is similar to the earthly world.
  • 司赏善罚恶之事 - 'Who oversees rewarding good deeds and punishing evil,' referring to a divine function.
  • 自陈姓名 - 'Presented my name,' indicating self-identification.
  • 将生平修善不报之事一一诉知 - 'Reported in detail the matters of good deeds I performed in life that went unrewarded.'
  • 责神无灵 - 'Accused the gods of being powerless or without spirit.'
  • 非我所知，亦非我所司 - 'This is not within my knowledge or my jurisdiction,' indicating a transfer of responsibility.
  • 何神所司 - 'Which god is in charge of this?'
  • 素大王 - 'The King of Su' or 'the King of Number,' likely a reference to a deity associated with fate or numbers.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孝廉钟悟 - 'Zhong Wu, the filial and upright man' – '孝廉' is a Han dynasty title for a recommended candidate for official office, indicating a man of virtue.
  • 常州人 - 'A man from Changzhou' – Changzhou is a city in present-day Jiangsu Province.
  • 冥王 - 'King of Hell' or 'the King of the Underworld,' a term used in Chinese religious and folk beliefs to refer to the ruler of the afterlife.
  • 李大王 - 'King Li' – likely a reference to a deity associated with justice or moral judgment.
  • 素大王 - 'King Su' – possibly a deity associated with fate or numbers, given the wordplay in the text.
  • 行善 - 'To perform good deeds' – central to Confucian and Daoist moral philosophy.
  • 修善不报 - 'Good deeds that went unrewarded' – a common concern in classical Chinese literature and religious texts, reflecting a belief in karma or divine justice.
  • 神无灵 - 'The gods are powerless' – a critique or accusation against the gods, suggesting a lack of divine intervention or justice.
  • 生平 - 'Throughout one's life' – a phrase used to describe a person's entire life history or experiences.

Proper Nouns

  • 孝廉钟悟 - 'Zhong Wu, the filial and upright man' – a title and name, to be kept as is.
  • 常州 - 'Changzhou' – a city in Jiangsu Province, to be kept as is.
  • 冥王 - 'King of Hell' – a title referring to the ruler of the underworld.
  • 李大王 - 'King Li' – a title, likely referring to a deity.
  • 素大王 - 'King Su' – another title, likely referring to a deity associated with numbers or fate.
  • 李 - A name, likely the name of the deity or judge.
  • 素 - A name, likely the name of the deity or judge.

  • 最后有一人衮冕玉带，状若帝王，貌伟然而衣履尽湿 - Finally, there was a man in a ceremonial robe and jade belt, resembling a king, with a majestic appearance but his clothes and shoes were all wet.
  • 我，周昭王也 - I am King Zhao of Zhou.

  • 何以一传至我，而依例南征，无故为楚人溺死 - Why did it come down to me, and I followed the precedent of leading a southern campaign, only to be drowned by the people of Chu for no reason.
  • 幸有勇士辛游靡长臂多力，曳我尸起，归葬成周 - Fortunately, there was a brave warrior named Xin Youmi with long arms and great strength, who pulled my body up and buried me in Chengzhou.
  • 否则徒为江鱼所吞矣 - Otherwise, I would have been swallowed by the river fish.
  • 后虽有齐侯小白借端一问，亦不过虚应故事，草草完结 - Later, although Duke Huan of Qi asked about it as a pretext, it was just a superficial response, and the matter was hastily concluded.
  • 如此奇冤，二千年来绝无报应 - Such a strange injustice has had no retribution for two thousand years.
  • 望神替一查 - I hope you will look into it.
  • 李王唯唯 - King Li nodded in agreement.
  • 余鬼闻之，纷纷然俱有怒色 - The other ghosts heard this and all showed angry expressions.
  • 钟方悟世事不平者，尚有许大冤抑 - Zhong then realized that there are still so many injustices in the world.
  • 如我贫困，固是小事，气为之平 - Like my own poverty, which is trivial, and my anger subsided.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 后稷、公刘 - Houji and Gong Liu, legendary ancestors of the Zhou dynasty, known for their contributions to agriculture and virtue.
  • 楚人 - Refers to the people of Chu, an ancient state in China.
  • 齐侯小白 - Duke Huan of Qi, a historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 成周 - The capital of the Zhou dynasty, located in present-day Luoyang, Henan Province.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • This sequence describes a complex series of events: holding a seal, stamping a back, revealing a true form, falling to the ground, the death of a fetus, and the discovery of two small foxes within the fetus.

  • This sequence describes the act of throwing the foxes into the fire, leading to the resolution of the supernatural event and the strict one's survival.
  • This sequence describes the subsequent birth of twins with red marks on their heads, linking back to the earlier events.
  • This sequence describes the tragic chain of events following the birth of the twins, leading to the death of the wife, the strict one, and the ultimate failure of the family line.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '主人素贤，为迎其妻来侍汤药' introduces the host and his wife, but the structure is somewhat elliptical. It should be read as 'The host, who was always virtuous, brought his wife to care for him while he was ill.'
  • The phrase '未三日，卒' is elliptical and should be read as 'Not even three days had passed when he died.'
  • The phrase '已而苏，谓妻曰' is elliptical and should be read as 'Afterward, he revived and said to his wife.'
  • The sentence '吾不活矣，所以复苏者，冥府宽恩，许来相诀故也' is a complex explanation for his revival and should be read as 'I will not survive, but the reason I revived is because of the leniency of the underworld, which allowed me to come and say goodbye.'
  • The sentence '见青衣四人拉吾同行，云‘有人告发索命事’' describes a scene in the afterlife and should be read as 'I saw four men in green robes pulling me along and said, 'Someone has accused us of demanding a life.'
  • The sentence '心知阴界，因问：‘吾何罪？’' is a question asked in the afterlife and should be read as 'I knew I was in the underworld and asked, 'What crime have I committed?'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '长鬼被缚竹墩沈翰林厚余，少与友张姓同学读书。' introduces the protagonist and his friend, but the structure is somewhat ambiguous. It can be read as 'Se, a tall ghost bound to a bamboo stand, was a翰林 named Houyu, who had studied with a friend named Zhang when they were young.'
  • The sentence '数日张不至，问之，张患伤寒甚剧，因往问候。' is somewhat elliptical, but it can be expanded to 'After several days, Zhang did not come. Se asked about him and learned that Zhang was suffering from a severe case of typhoid fever, so he went to visit him.'
  • The sentence '入门悄然，将升堂，见堂上先有一长人端坐，仰面视堂上题额。' describes Se entering the house and seeing a tall figure sitting in the hall, looking up at the inscription on the hall.
  • The dialogue '沈叩以“何处来？”长人云：“张某当死，余为勾差，当先来与其家堂神说明，再动手勾捉。”' is clear, but the structure can be expanded to 'Se asked, 'Where are you from?' The tall figure replied, 'Zhang is about to die. I am here to deliver a summons from the underworld. I must first explain this to the household's ancestral spirits before I can proceed to capture him.'
  • The sentence '沈以张“寡母在堂，未娶无子，胡可以死？”恳画计缓之。' is somewhat elliptical but can be expanded to 'Se said, 'Zhang's mother is still alive, he has not married, and he has no children. How can he die?' He then requested a plan to delay Zhang's fate.'

  • The phrase '某立帐房于路旁' is straightforward but could be interpreted as 'the general set up a tent on the side of the road.'

  • The phrase '妇力辩是尼非僧，夫不信，始以詈骂，继加捶楚' is elliptical and could be read as 'the wife strongly defended that it was a nun, not a monk, but the husband did not believe her, so he started scolding her and then beat her.'
  • The phrase '遍告邻佑' is straightforward but could be interpreted as 'he informed all the neighbors.'
  • The phrase '邻佑以事在昏夜，各推不知' is elliptical and could be read as 'the neighbors, considering it happened at night, each claimed they did not know.'
  • The phrase '妇不胜其冤，竟缢死' is elliptical and could be read as 'the woman could not bear the injustice and ultimately hanged herself.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 斗姥阁 - 'Doumu Pavilion' – a structure dedicated to the goddess Doumu (斗姥), a deity associated with the North Star and the heavens in Chinese cosmology. Such pavilions were often built as acts of merit or to appease deities.

  • 券 - 'Voucher' or 'deed' – a legal document used in transactions, especially in land or religious donations.
  • 伪辞让 - 'Falsely declining' – a common rhetorical device in classical Chinese, where a person pretends to refuse something in order to appear modest or sincere, often in the context of receiving a gift or honor.
  • 心悟其奸 - 'Realizing the deceit' – a psychological moment of understanding, a common motif in classical Chinese storytelling.
  • 家尚有余 - 'Still had wealth' – a reference to the financial stability of the family, which may reflect the social class of the protagonist.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 少顷，外有敲门者，丁惧，伏床下 - This sequence describes a series of actions in a compact form: 'After a short while, someone is knocking on the door outside, and Ding is afraid, so he crouches under the bed.'
  • 牛头鬼入，取牛头掷于几上，一一假面具也 - This sequence describes the ghost entering, taking off its mask, and revealing its true form: 'The bull-headed ghost enters, takes off its bull head, and throws it onto the table, revealing that it was a fake mask.'
  • 既去面具，眉目言笑，宛若平人 - This sequence describes the ghost removing its mask and appearing as an ordinary person: 'After removing the mask, its facial expressions and speech are as if it were an ordinary person.'
  • 谓其妻曰：“惫甚！今日侍阎王审大案数十，脚跟立久酸痛，须斟酒饮我。” - This sequence describes the ghost speaking to its wife about its exhaustion: 'It says to its wife, 'I'm extremely tired! Today, I served Yama in reviewing dozens of major cases, and my heels ache from standing for so long. I need to pour me some wine.'
  • 徐惊曰：“有生人气！”且嗅且寻 - This sequence describes the ghost gradually realizing that there is a living person present: 'It gradually exclaims, 'There's a living person's smell!' and both smells and searches.'
  • 妻度不可隐，拉丁出，叩头告之故，代为哀求 - This sequence describes the wife realizing that she cannot hide the situation and pulling Ding out to beg for mercy: 'The wife realizes that she cannot hide the situation, pulls Ding out, kneels and bows to tell the reason, and begs on his behalf.'
  • 是人非独为妻故将救之，是实于我有德 - This sequence explains why the ghost will save Ding: 'This person will not only be saved for the sake of his wife, but he truly has virtue toward me.'
  • 我在庙中蒙灰满面，此人为我拭净，是一长者 - This sequence describes the ghost's gratitude toward Ding: 'I was in the temple, covered in dust all over my face, and this person wiped it clean for me, he is a respectable person.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the narrator and sets the scene. It can be read as: 'A scholar from Changzhou named Ma Shilin, who himself says, when he was young, studied under his father in the north tower, and the window opened to a place close to the raised platform of the elderly man Wang who sold chrysanthemums.'
  • This sentence describes the morning scene. It can be read as: 'One day, early in the morning, leaning on the window and looking out, as the sky was slightly light, he saw the elderly man Wang climb the platform to water the chrysanthemums, finish, and was about to come down.'
  • This sentence describes the interaction between the elderly man and the man carrying manure. It can be read as: 'There was a man carrying two buckets who climbed the platform, intending to help with watering. The elderly man looked unhappy and refused him; but the man carrying the manure insisted on going up, and they squeezed each other on the slope of the platform.'
  • This sentence describes the accident. It can be read as: 'It rained, making the platform slippery, and the slope was steep and high. The elderly man pushed the man carrying the manure with his hand, and the upper and lower positions were unequal in strength, so he lost his footing and fell off the platform.'
  • This sentence describes the aftermath of the accident. It can be read as: 'The elderly man hurried to help him up, but he did not get up, and the two buckets pressed against his chest, and his feet suddenly went straight.'
  • This sentence describes the elderly man's actions after the accident. It can be read as: 'The elderly man was greatly frightened, stayed silent and did not speak, pulled the foot of the man carrying the manure, opened the back door, placed him on the riverbank, then lifted the buckets and placed them beside the body, returned home, closed the door, and lay down again.'
  • This sentence describes the spread of news. It can be read as: 'As the day progressed, he heard rumors spreading widely that there was a dead person on the riverbank, and the village head reported to the official.'
  • This sentence describes the arrival of the official. It can be read as: 'At noon, the magistrate of Wujin County arrived, beating a gong.'
  • This sentence describes the forensic examination. It can be read as: 'The forensic expert knelt and reported: 'The body has no injuries, it is a fall from a height.''
  • This sentence describes the investigation. It can be read as: 'The official asked the neighbors, and they all claimed they did not know.'
  • This sentence describes the official handling of the body. It can be read as: 'He then ordered the coffin to be prepared and sealed, displayed a notice to find the family of the deceased, and left.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 捉鬼 - Literal phrase meaning 'to catch a ghost'; used to describe the act of confronting and capturing a supernatural entity.
  • 夜梦魇 - Refers to a nightmare or a bad dream experienced at night.
  • 寤 - Means to wake up or become aware.
  • 逼帷立 - Describes a ghost standing close to the curtains, creating an eerie presence.
  • 素勇 - Indicates that the person is naturally brave or courageous.

  • 急夺门走 - Means the ghost hastily tried to escape through the door.
  • 误触墙 - Indicates that the ghost accidentally bumped into the wall.
  • 状甚狼狈 - Describes the ghost's appearance as very disheveled or distressed.
  • 欲喊集家人 - Means to want to call for family members.
  • 声噤不能出 - Indicates that the voice was paralyzed and could not be heard.
  • 极力大叫 - Describes the effort to shout loudly.
  • 鬼形缩小如婴儿 - Means the ghost's form shrank to the size of an infant.
  • 各持炬来照 - Describes family members holding torches to illuminate the scene.
  • 坏丝绵一团 - Refers to a clump of damaged silk padding.
  • 窗外瓦砾乱掷如雨 - Describes debris being thrown outside like rain.
  • 鬼党虚吓人耳 - Means the ghost's companions were trying to scare people with false threats.
  • 奚能为 - A rhetorical question meaning 'what can they do?'
  • 倘释之，将助为祟 - Indicates that if the ghost is released, it might help others cause mischief.
  • 不如杀一鬼以惩百鬼 - Suggests that it is better to kill one ghost to punish many.
  • 左手握鬼，右手取家人火炬烧之 - Describes the action of holding the ghost with one hand and burning it with a torch from the family.
  • 膊有声 - Refers to the sound of flesh burning.
  • 鲜血迸射 - Describes blood splattering.
  • 臭气不可闻 - Indicates that the smell was so strong that it could not be ignored.
  • 地上血厚寸许 - Means the blood on the ground was about an inch thick.
  • 腥腻如胶 - Describes the blood as sticky and viscous.

  • 婺源汪启明 - Refers to Wang Qiming from Wuyuan, a place known for its historical and cultural significance.
  • 迁居上河之进士第 - Indicates that Wang Qiming moved to a house in Shanghe that was once an imperial examination hall.
  • 汪进士波故宅 - Refers to the former residence of Wang Jinshi Bo, a past scholar-official.
  • 乾隆甲午四月 - Refers to the month of April in the year 1774, during the reign of Emperor Qianlong.
  • 王葑亭舍人 - Refers to Wang Fengting, a writer who composed a poem about the incident.
